coredata.dc:title: Radiolytically prepared blue photoluminescent Silicon Oxide nanocomposites for highly selective sensing of picric acid: Mechanism and development of paper strip-based detection
coredata.dc:creator: Guleria, Apurav
Rath, Madhab C.
Adhikari, Soumyakanti
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we wish to report highly selective sensing of picric acid (PA), one of the primary explosive ingredients as well as a well-known environmental hazard in 100 % water medium. For the said purpose, radiolytically synthesized blue photoluminescent PEGylated silicon oxide nanocomposites (PEG@SiONCs) with ∼49 % quantum efficiency (QE) were used as fluorescence probe. The limit of detection (LOD) of PA was determined to be ∼0.16 μM in the 2–85 μM range. Substantial reduction in blue photoluminescence of PEG@SiONCs, and shift in the emission colour from blue towards green in presence of specific concentrations of PA, are the interesting highlights of the present work. This colour change can be utilized to confirm the presence of PA in the sample. Mechanistic investigations involving temperature-dependent steady-state photoluminescence, and PA concentration-dependent lifetime studies revealed inner filter effect (IFE) based photoluminescence quenching mechanism. In addition, the interference studies carried out in the presence of 21 other organic/inorganic compounds demonstrated the highly selective sensing of PA by PEG@SiONCs. The PA sensing ability of the nanocomposites was also tested in the pH window ranging from 2 to 12. Meanwhile, paper strips were prepared using PEGylated SiONCs, and samples containing various metal ions and organic compounds were tested to examine their suitability for real-time detection of PA.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic depiction of PA sensing by utilizing paper strips prepared from radiolytically synthesized blue photoluminescent PEG@SiONCs under a hand-held UV lamp. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Effect of various compounds (generally used in explosives) on the PL intensity of PEG@SiONCs. The concentration of compounds in the respective sample solutions was 110 μM, while the concentration of PEG@SiONCs was 0.02 g/mL.

(A) PL quenching of PEG@SiONCs as a function of PA concentration; (B) Digital photograph of the colloidal solution of PEG@SiONCs in the (a) absence and (b) presence of PA (126.2 μM) under UV light. The pale green PL could be seen from cuvette (b) containing PA at a particular concentration; (C) Stern–Volmer plot for PL quenching of PEG@SiONCs, i.e., F0/F versus [PA, μM] (λex = 340 nm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Plot of F0/F versus [PA, μM] at different temperatures (λex = 340 nm); (b) Temporal PL decay curves (λex = 339 nm; λem = 430 nm) for PEG@SiONCs in the presence of quencher [i.e., PA, μM] with different concentrations.

UV–Visible absorption spectra of the colloidal solution of PA [1], PEG@SiONCs [2], and PEG@SiONCs + PA [3]. The PL excitation spectrum of PEG@SiONCs [4] is also provided to show the overlap with the absorption spectrum of the quencher (i.e., PA).
FESEM images of PEG@SiONCs in the absence (a) and presence of PA (b).

Histogram showing variations in PL of PEG@SiONCs in the presence of various metal ions, and compounds containing different functional groups at varying concentrations. The composition of Sol X, Sol Y, Sol Z, Sol P, & Sol Q is already mentioned in experimental section 2.3. The control indicates PL intensity for PEG@SiONCs without any interfering entity. Concentration ratios between interfering species and PA were maintained keeping the former either twice or equal to the latter.
Plot showing the effect of different pH conditions on the Stern-Volmer constant (KSV) values.

Digital photographs (under UV light) illustrating the response of PEG@SiONCs coated paper strips to varying PA concentrations, both in the absence and presence of compounds containing various functional groups as interfering entities. The number markings are explained as follows. (1) PEG@SiONCs coated paper as a reference. Nanocomposite coated papers were drop-cast with solution having following PA concentration: (2) 12.4 μM, (3) 24.9 μM, (4) 49.7 μM, (5) 74.4 μM, (6) 99.0 μM, (7) 123.4 μM, (8) 147.7 μM, (9) 172.0 μM, (10) 240.0 μM, (11) 291.3 μM. Nanocomposite coated papers were drop-cast with a solution of –NO2 containing compounds (i.e., Sol Y) at effective concentrations of (12) 55 μM, (13) 104.7 μM, (14) 150.0 μM, (15) 191.0 μM, (16) 229.2 μM. The label (17) represents nanocomposite coated paper drop-cast with Sol Y (effective concentration of each compound ∼ 229.2 μM) along with PA (with an effective concentration of 240.0 μM).
Stern-Volmer constant (KSV) and quenching rate constant (Kq) values at different temperatures.
Stern-Volmer constant (KSV) and quenching rate constant (Kq) values at different pH conditions.
Determination of PA in actual water samples using PEG@SiONCs

head.ce:title: Radiolytically prepared blue photoluminescent Silicon Oxide nanocomposites for highly selective sensing of picric acid: Mechanism and development of paper strip-based detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we wish to report highly selective sensing of picric acid (PA), one of the primary explosive ingredients as well as a well-known environmental hazard in 100 % water medium. For the said purpose, radiolytically synthesized blue photoluminescent PEGylated silicon oxide nanocomposites (PEG@SiONCs) with ∼49 % quantum efficiency (QE) were used as fluorescence probe. The limit of detection (LOD) of PA was determined to be ∼0.16 μM in the 2–85 μM range. Substantial reduction in blue photoluminescence of PEG@SiONCs, and shift in the emission colour from blue towards green in presence of specific concentrations of PA, are the interesting highlights of the present work. This colour change can be utilized to confirm the presence of PA in the sample. Mechanistic investigations involving temperature-dependent steady-state photoluminescence, and PA concentration-dependent lifetime studies revealed inner filter effect (IFE) based photoluminescence quenching mechanism. In addition, the interference studies carried out in the presence of 21 other organic/inorganic compounds demonstrated the highly selective sensing of PA by PEG@SiONCs. The PA sensing ability of the nanocomposites was also tested in the pH window ranging from 2 to 12. Meanwhile, paper strips were prepared using PEGylated SiONCs, and samples containing various metal ions and organic compounds were tested to examine their suitability for real-time detection of PA.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sensors have become increasingly indispensable in our daily routines and continue to play a pivotal role in various aspects of modern life [1,2]. Presently, the primary areas of concern for global challenges are centered around three major domains, namely energy, health, and the environment. In order to improve the growth of said sectors, the implementation of sensors with a high level of efficiency is deemed desirable. Undoubtedly, researchers worldwide have invested significant efforts in the past and present toward the development of highly efficient sensors. For example, the expeditious and uncomplicated identification of toxic/hazardous chemicals in aquatic environments that is both sensitive and selective has garnered significant interest, given the crucial significance of this issue [1].

One such family of hazardous chemicals is nitroaromatics. Amongst these, picric acid (PA), also known as 2, 4, 6-trinitrophenol according to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), is a notable nitroaromatic compound that is commonly utilized as an explosive material in the production of rocket fuel, matches, fireworks, and as a sensitizer in photography [3,4]. Furthermore, it serves as an ingredient in a number of industries including the leather, pharmaceutical, chemical, and dye sectors. An important property of PA is its fairly good water solubility (∼1.4 g/100 ml), coupled with its electron-deficient nature, which renders its degradation within the biosphere exceedingly challenging. No wonder, it has a propensity to easily pollute soil and groundwater, and enter the food chain [4,5]. Moreover, the presence of phenolic and nitro functionalities in PA may lead to plausible health implications. It has been observed that long exposure to PA elicits strong ocular and dermal irritation, as well as respiratory ailments and hepatic impairment. Additional consequences of exposure to PA include harm to blood, liver, and kidney cells, as well as the potential to induce cancer, anaemia, cyanosis, and male infertility [3–5]. Thus, the precise detection of PA in the presence of structurally analogous and other interfering compounds holds significant importance in healthcare, forensics, and environmental domains.

Of course, there are several analytical methods/techniques for the detection of PA [3,4,6,7]. Amongst these, the most commonly used ones are based on electrochemical methods [3,4,7,8], mass spectrometry [3,4,9], high-performance liquid chromatography [4,7,9], nuclear quadrupole resonance [7,10], and Raman spectroscopy [4,11]. However, the majority of these methodologies require expensive instrumentation and expertise, extended analysis periods, arduous sample preparation, and are not easily applicable for on-site analysis. Hence, developing a simple, rapid, cost-effective sensor with high selectivity and low detection limit for PA is highly desirable.

In this perspective, ﬂuorescent probes offer numerous benefits, including enhanced sensitivity and selectivity, economical, and rapid response. No wonder, several fluorophores such as dyes, polymers, Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) and other organic molecules have been used for the sensing of PA and other nitroaromatic compounds [4–6,12–27]. Various PL-based mechanisms have been reported for their sensing such as static quenching, dynamic quenching, photoinduced electron transfer (PET), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), etc. In recent times, photoluminescent nanomaterials (carbon-based, II-VI quantum dots (CdSe, CdTe, core-shell), metal-based, e.g., Cu) have also been explored for PA sensing [5,6,12,13,28–31]. Certainly, while all these luminophores come with their own set of advantages and disadvantages, many of them do not align well with “green” principles, as they are inherently toxic, costly, possess low photostability, necessitate extended synthesis periods, subject to limitations in scaling up, and require the use of harsh experimental conditions. However, given the growing emphasis on the use of “green” materials and adopting environmentally-friendly synthetic methodologies/protocols, the challenge lies in the development of a photoluminescent probes/sensors that exhibit sustainable attributes, including cost-effectiveness, non-toxic, wide-abundance, and an environmentally-friendly synthesis process that is time-efficient and facilitates scalability.

Herein, we explored photoluminescent Silicon (Si)-based nanocomposites for PA sensing. Since the discovery of quantum confinement induced luminescence in porous Si in 1991 [32], applications of its nanomaterials have attracted considerable interest in modern technologies. Moreover, Nano-Si offers various advantages over other nanomaterials owing to the wide-abundance, non-toxic nature and cost-effectiveness of Si itself [33,34]. In the current work, the nanocomposites of Si were synthesized by radiation-mediated approach involving high energy electron-beam sourced from a linear accelerator (LINAC) [35]. It is noteworthy that this approach embodies several environmentally friendly aspects [36–45]. This technique does not necessitate the use of external toxic chemicals, as the reduction of precursors is accomplished through the generation of solvated electrons (with a reduction potential of approximately −2.9 eV) or other radicals (such as hydroxyl radicals, •OH, with a redox potential of approximately +2.8 eV) under specific conditions. Moreover, through electron-beam (EB) assisted methodology, the fabrication of nanomaterials can be accomplished within a matter of seconds, depending upon the absorbed dose [35,44,45]. The utilization of radiation-mediated methodology also presents a notable benefit in the scale-up production of nanomaterials using industrial accelerators that are commonly employed for diverse commercial applications, including but not limited to polymer crosslinking, medical kit sterilization, and other related purposes. Besides, these PEGylated Silicon oxide nanocomposites (PEG@SiONCs) were found to exhibit insignificant cytotoxicity, which could be attributed to their PEG coating [35]. Nonetheless, in light of the aforementioned environmentally friendly and sustainable features integrated into this approach, we hereby present the utilization of radiolytically prepared blue photoluminescent PEG@SiONCs for highly selective detection of PA in aqueous solutions containing various other structural analogues and potentially interfering ions. Various steady-state and time-dependent photoluminescence (PL) studies have been carried out to investigate the sensing mechanism. Furthermore, the robustness of this sensing platform was demonstrated through temperature and pH-dependent PL studies. In order to assess the feasibility of real-time detection, a paper-based kit was fabricated utilizing PEGylated SiONCs. This kit was employed to visually examine the presence of PA in the presence of various nitroaromatic and metal ions using a hand-held UV lamp.

High purity chemicals, such as polyethylene glycol-10000 (PEG-10K; purity ≥99 %) and (3-Aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTS, purity ≥97 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, and Alfa Aesar (UK), respectively. High purity (≥99.9 %) Nitrous oxide (N2O) gas used for bubbling the sample solutions before irradiation was purchased from Alchemie Gases & Chemicals Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India. The sample solutions were prepared using nanopure water, also known as double-distilled water (conductivity, 0.06 μScm−1), which was obtained from a Millipore water purifying system. Various Nitro (‒NO2) and other functional group containing organic/inorganic compounds were used for sensing experiments, i.e., Urea nitrate (UN), Acetonitrile (AN), Acetone (AE), Formaldehyde (FM), KNO3 (KN), Thiourea (TU), Phenol (PH), Hydrazine hydrate (HH), Citric acid (CA), Urea (UA), Ammonium Perchlorate (AP), Benzoic acid (BA), Picric acid (PA), 2-Nitrobenzonitrile (2NB), 4-Nitro-1H-pyrazole (NPZ), Nitromethane (NM), 2,4-Dinitrobenzoic acid (DNB), Nitrobenzene (NB), 2-Nitrophenol (2NP), 3,5-Dinitro-4-hydroxyphenylacetic acid (DNHPA), 4-Nitrophenol (4NP), and 4-Nitroaniline (4NA). Table S1 contains the structures of Nitro (‒NO2) and other organic/inorganic compounds containing functional groups. Metal salts such as ZnSO4.7H2O, AgNO3, CaCl2.2H2O, CrCl3, CoSO4.7H2O, CuSO4.5H2O, CdSO4, FeSO4.7H2O, Hg2Cl2, MnSO4.7H2O, NiSO4.6H2O, PbCl2, and NaNO3 were also used as interfering agents in the sensing experiments.

The method employed for the synthesis of PEG@SiONCs involved the utilization of electron beam irradiation, as has been reported in our earlier work [35]. Briefly, the methodology involved the preparation of sample solutions through the dissolution of PEG-10K solution (with a concentration of ∼0.6 g/mL) in double-distilled water (NPW), followed by the addition of an optimized quantity of APTS. The resultant solution (labeled as Solution A) was purged with N2O gas and subsequently irradiated with 7 MeV electron beam (FWHM ∼ 2 μs) sourced from the LINAC. Fig. 1 provides a schematic representation of the synthetic procedure. The expeditious formation of PEG@SiONCs is a salient observation, as it occurred within few seconds of sample irradiation. The nanocomposites were extracted by freeze-drying, which were washed with acetone 3–4 times. The NCs were well-characterized and found to have an average size of ∼103 nm (determined by TEM measurements), while the average hydrodynamic size was determined to be ∼145 nm from the DLS size distribution. Apparently, it indicated the formation of a PEG shell on the as-prepared silicon oxide-based nanocomposites. The FTIR and TGA studies confirmed the formation of PEG coating over the NCs. Also, the presence of Si─O─Si units were evident from the IR peaks at 696 and 1030 cm−1 [35]. The findings from various such compositional studies confirmed the formation of silicon oxide nanocomposites (SiOx<2). Nonetheless, as obtained PEG@SiONCs exhibited blue PL (maximum quantum efficiency ∼49 %), as could be seen from Fig. 1.

To conduct sensing experiments, the concentration of PEG@SiONCs was optimized to 20 mg/mL (by dissolving 1g of PEG@SiONCs powder in 50 mL of double-distilled water using sonication method). The optimization was carried out considering both colloidal stability and achieving the highest PL intensity. The robust colloidal stability of PEG@SiONCs solution at this concentration is demonstrated in Fig. S1. Nonetheless, the resultant solution with optimized NCs concentration was labeled as Solution B. The solutions of various Nitro (‒NO2) and other functional group containing organic/inorganic compounds (i.e., PA, UN, AN, AE, FM, KN, TU, PH, HH, CA, UA, AP, BA, 2NB, NPZ, NM, DNB, NB, 2NP, DNHPA, 4NP, and 4NA) were prepared with a stock concentration of 10 mM by dissolving them in double-distilled water. The PL quenching of Solution B, which is a colloidal solution of PEG@SiONCs, was subsequently observed while being exposed to various such compounds individually. To achieve the aforementioned objective, a volume of 20 μL of a specified stock solution containing an organic or inorganic compound (as previously indicated) was introduced into 1.8 mL of Solution B. After simple mixing in a cuvette, the PL of the resulting solution was promptly assessed, utilizing an excitation wavelength (λex) of 340 nm.

In the interference studies, various set of solutions were tested. These solutions were labeled as follows: Sol X (solution of PEG@SiONCs along with a mixture of various functional groups compounds excluding –NO2, i.e., UN, AN, AE, FM, KN, TU, PH, HH, CA, UA, AP, BA), Sol Y (solution of PEG@SiONCs along with a mixture of –NO2 containing compounds, i.e., 2NB, NPZ, NM, DNB, NB, 2NP, DNHPA), Sol Z (solution of PEG@SiONCs along with a mixture of various metal ions, i.e., Hg+, Ni2+, K+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Na+, Co2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Cr2+, Fe2+), Sol P (solution of PEG@SiONCs and 4NA), Sol Q (solution of PEG@SiONCs and 4NP), Sol R (solution of PEG@SiONCs and Cr(VI) ions).
It is to be noted that all the experiments related to PA sensing using PEG@SiONCs were carried out at room temperature conditions (25 °C).

The UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured utilizing a JASCO V-650 absorption spectrophotometer at room temperature. The JASCO FP-8500 spectrofluorometer was used to record steady-state PL measurements. The PL lifetime measurements were performed using a time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) instrument manufactured by IBH in the United Kingdom. The measurement of the Instrument Response Function (IRF) was conducted by using a suspension of TiO2 in water. The samples were excited using LED sources emitting wavelengths of 292 and 339 nm. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were acquired using the Libra 200FE instrument. The irradiation of the samples was carried out using 7 MeV electron pulses sourced from a linear electron accelerator (obtained from Viritech Ltd., U.K) [46]. The dosimetry experiment was conducted by utilizing an air-saturated solution with a concentration of 5 × 10−2 mol dm−3 KSCN assuming Gε for
( SCN )
2
• −
= 2.6 × 10−4 m2 J−1 [35,46].

The PEG@SiONCs prepared through radiolytic route exhibited blue PL with the highest intensity observed at ∼425 nm accompanied by a shoulder peak at ∼480 nm [35]. In the course of conducting exploratory experiments to assess the suitability of PEG@SiONCs as a sensing platform for nitroaromatic compounds, it was noted that these nanocomposites exhibited the most significant decrease in their PL intensity when exposed to PA. Fig. 2 demonstrates that PA as an analyte exhibited the highest degree of PL quenching, exceeding 85 %. Conversely, the majority of other organic/inorganic and nitroaromatic compounds displayed minimal PL quenching. Indeed, the phenomenon of PL quenching has been observed in the case of DNHPA and 4NA. However, there exists a significant difference in the quenching efficiencies between these analytes, amounting to only ∼60 %, when compared to that of PA (exceeding 85 %).

Fig. 3 A displays the PL spectra of PEG@SiONCs as a variable of PA concentration. It is evident that the PL intensity of the nanocomposites displayed a consistent decrease as the concentration of PA increased. The PL intensity exhibited a significant reduction (∼50 %) when the concentration of PA reached or exceeded 45 μM. It is noteworthy that with an increase in PA concentration, not only the PL was quenched, but the peak position also appeared to red-shift. The observed phenomenon may be attributed to the significant reduction in the PL peak at ∼425 nm, in contrast to the shoulder peak at ∼480 nm, as the concentration of PA increases. Consequently, the reduction in the 425 nm peak gave the impression of a new emerging peak, accompanied by a red-shift. Nonetheless, the emission colour of PEG@SiONCs undergoes a transition from blue to green when exposed to PA, albeit with a decrease in PL intensity. In fact, digital photograph (Fig. 3B) of the colloidal solution of PEG@SiONCs in absence and presence of PA under UV light confirms this observation. The pale green PL was observed emanating from the cuvette that contained a specific concentration of PA. This observation can be utilized in the visual detection of PA, in addition to the “PL turn-off” effect. Fig. 3C shows a plot of F0/F versus [PA, μM]. The terms F0 and F represent the PL intensity of PEG@SiONCs in the absence (control) and presence of quencher “Q” (i.e., PA), respectively. It can be seen that the ratio F0/F varies linearly with PA concentration in the 2–85 μM range.

The limit of detection (LOD) was evaluated using 3σ/S [47] and determined to be 0.16 μM (RSD ∼ 7 %). The term “σ” is the standard deviation of the blank (no quencher) signal and “S” is the slope of the linear calibration plot. As previously stated, several luminophores [4–6,12–31,48–54] have been explored for their potential in sensing of PA and other nitroaromatic compounds. In Table S2, a comprehensive list of the LOD values including their respective ranges, for PA sensing utilizing these luminophores have been presented. Of course, some of the works have reported lower detection limits. However, employing Si-based NCs as sensors presents several inherent benefits attributed to silicon, including its non-toxicity, cost-effectiveness, and eco-friendliness. Additionally, the utilization of a radiolytic approach for their synthesis, as highlighted in the introduction, offers added advantages such as scalability, facileness, time-efficient synthesis (takes place in few seconds), no requirement of external toxic reducing agents, and other harsh conditions (i.e., high temperature and pressure). Nevertheless, efforts are being made in our laboratory to enhance the sensitivity and lower the detection limit for PA sensing.

To gain insights of the sensing mechanism, the PL quenching data could be analysed by Stern-Volmer equation, as follows [55]:

The terms F0 and F have been explained earlier also and represent the PL intensity of PEG@SiONCs in the absence (control) and presence of quencher “Q” (i.e., PA), respectively. While, Ksv and [Q] are the Stern–Volmer constant and quencher concentration, respectively. As mentioned earlier, the plot of F0/F versus [PA, μM] (Fig. 3C) shows good linearity in the range 2–85 μM. This could be an indication of the presence of static or dynamic quenching mechanism involved in PA sensing. However, further analysis has been carried out to ascertain the most probable mechanism. The slope obtained from the linear fitting of the data shown in the plot of F0/F versus [PA, μM] (Fig. 3C), gave the value of Ksv as 2.5 × 104 M−1 (R2 = 0.989). As can be seen from Fig. 4b, the PL decay traces were almost unaffected in the presence of PA at different levels of concentration ranging from 5 to 126.2 μM.

The average lifetime values (<τ>, ns) of nanocomposites in the presence of PA with different concentrations are provided in Table S3. Even at high PA concentration of 126.2 μM, the average lifetime value (4.8 ns) was found to be same as that of PEG@SiONCs (in the absence of PA).

Based on these observations, it can be stated that the observed reduction of PEG@SiONCs PL in the presence of PA is not significantly influenced either by static or dynamic quenching-based mechanisms. However, an alternative quenching mechanism, distinct from the static or dynamic processes, could be anticipated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inner filter effect (IFE) as the sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: For the sensing of various hazardous nitroaromatic compounds such as PA, novel fluoroprobes have been developed, predominantly employing FRET and PET based sensing mechanisms. For example, aggregation induced emission (AIE) active probes were developed by Wang et al. [20] and Majeed et al. [23] for PA sensing. The latter group achieved remarkably low LOD values, reaching as low as 2 nM, and attributed the sensing mechanism to PET and FRET. Shanmugaraju et al. [15] synthesized several π-electron rich fluorescent aromatic compounds containing trimethylsilylethynyl functionality as selective fluorescence sensors for the detection of electron-deficient nitroaromatics. The mechanism was ascribed to charge-transfer (CT) complex formation between these compounds. However, in addition to well-established sensing mechanisms such as static and dynamic quenching, FRET, and PET, IFE has gained considerable attention recently due to its flexibility and straightforwardness. In essence, IFE occurs as a result of the absorption of excitation or emission light by an absorber or quencher present in the sample [56,57]. Apparently, in this phenomenon, the absorption spectrum of the absorber or quencher exhibits a degree of overlap with the fluorescence excitation or emission spectra of the luminophore. Unlike other mechanisms, IFE doesn't necessitate specific orientation, distance, linkage, or interaction conditions between the fluorophore and absorber [56,57]. Importantly, the IFE is distinct from both static and dynamic quenching processes [56,57]. Key methods to identify IFE include: (i) comparing excitation/emission spectra with the absorption profile of absorber; (ii) no change in the excited state lifetime of luminophore in the presence or absence of the absorber; (iii) no change in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the luminophore in the presence of the absorber. In static quenching, interaction between the luminophore and absorber/quencher occurs in the ground state, causing a reduction in PL. The main identification methods for static quenching include: (i) changes in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the luminophore upon interaction with the absorber; (ii) noting a gradual gain in PL with an increase in temperature due to reduced interaction between luminophore and absorber. While, dynamic quenching is a result of the excited fluorescent probe colliding with the quencher in a diffusion-controlled manner [55–57], leading to charge or energy transfer. The primary identification methods for dynamic quenching involve: (i) changes in the PL lifetime of luminophore in the presence of the absorber; (ii) an increase in dynamic quenching effect with rising temperature due to enhanced collisional quenching in the excited state.

In contemporary times, the IFE has demonstrated its efficacy and efficiency as a strategic tool for transforming alterations in analytical absorption signals into corresponding perturbations in fluorescence signals [35,56,57]. Consequently, IFE-based assays have substantially enhanced the sensitivity and selectivity of the sensing mechanism for various analytes such as ions (e.g., Cr (VI), Cu2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Hg+, etc.), small molecules (e.g., melamine, ascorbic acid, dopamine, organophosphorus pesticides, uric acid, etc.) and macromolecules (e.g., Haemoglobin, Heparin, Lysozyme, IgG, b-glucuronidase, etc.) [56–58]. The detection limit of these analytes has been (generally) reported to be in the range of μM to pM. Recently, IFE has been explored as a predominant mechanism for sensing of PA. For instance, Li et al. [59] reported PA sensing with a detection limit of 0.3 μM by using 1-Aminopyrene@ZIF-8 composite as the fluorescent probe. Zhang et al. [13] used fluorescent copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) for detection of PA with an LOD of 0.98 μM. Of course, there have been reports in which researchers have reported lower detection limits for PA of the scale of nM concentration, with IFE as the primary sensing mechanism [14,60–62].

As stated previously, the present work emphasizes on the advantageous aspects of radiation-derived PEGylated silicon oxide nanocomposites, which incorporate sustainability prospects (such as scalability, eco-friendly, economic) of the synthetic methodology as well as the sensing platform. Subsequent investigations revealed the involvement of IFE-based PL quenching mechanism as the effective process in PA sensing. The predominant role of IFE behind the PA sensing ability of PEG@SiONCs could be realized from the absorption spectrum of PA, and the PL excitation spectrum of PEG@SiONCs (shown in Fig. 5 ). It is evident that the excitation spectrum of PEG@SiONCs lies completely within the absorption region of PA. Apart from this, there is also an overlap between the absorption spectral region of PA and the PL spectrum of PEG@SiONCs (ranging from 350 to 650 nm with maximum intensity at ∼ 425 nm, depicted in Fig. 3A). In such circumstances, differentiating IFE from FRET becomes challenging. Nonetheless, the considerable reduction or quenching of PEG@SiONCs PL in the presence of PA is attributed to IFE. This is supported by two main observations. As can be seen in Fig. 5, the UV–Vis absorption spectral profile of the fluorophore (PEG@SiONCs) remained unchanged in the presence of PA, with the exception of the emergence of an additional shoulder peak (corresponding to PA) in the absorption spectrum of PEG@SiONCs + PA. Another noteworthy observation is the negligible change in the average lifetime values of PEG@SiONCs in the presence of PA (even at higher concentrations), as can be seen from Fig. 4b and Table S3. As mentioned earlier, it is a well-established fact that, in general, the PL lifetime of the luminophore undergoes alteration in the case of FRET, but remains same during IFE [56,57].

FESEM investigations were also performed to assess the possibility of an interaction between PEG@SiONCs and PA, which, if present, could potentially be identified through alterations in the morphology of former. Fig. 6 shows the FESEM images of PEG@SiONCs in the absence and presence of PA. In the absence of PA, the NCs appeared as spherical-shaped agglomerated structures, as depicted in Fig. 6a, which could be attributed to PEGylation. The morphology of the NCs remains same even in the presence of PA, as evident in Fig. 6b. This observation suggests the absence of any interaction between the NCs and PA which could have caused the PL quenching, thus reinforcing the role of IFE in the sensing mechanism.

Apart from low detection limit, the selectivity of the sensor towards the analyte is a crucial parameter in its development. Hence, it is of utmost importance to conduct a comprehensive investigation on the possible interference (in PA sensing by PEG@SiONCs) anticipated from the presence of various organic/inorganic compounds (containing nitro and other functional groups) in the matrix. This study aims to evaluate the efficacy and suitability of PEG@SiONCs for their potential application as a PA sensor. The histogram in Fig. 7 shows the change in PL of PEG@SiONCs in the presence of various metal ions, and compounds containing different functional groups as interfering species at various levels of concentration. The concentration of interfering species (organic/inorganic compounds and metal ions) and PA was varied to maintain ratios wherein the former has either twice or equal concentration relative to the latter one. The labelling of the solutions containing different types of interfering entities are mentioned as: Sol X = Solution containing PEG@SiONCs along with a mixture of compounds containing various functional groups excluding –NO2 (UN, AN, AE, FM, KN, TU, PH, HH, CA, UA, AP, BA), Sol Y = Solution containing PEG@SiONCs along with a mixture of –NO2 containing compounds (2NB, NPZ, NM, DNB, NB, 2NP, DNHPA) excluding 4NP & 4NA, Sol Z = Solution containing PEG@SiONCs along with a mixture of various metal ions (Hg+, Ni2+, K+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Na+, Co2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Cr2+, Fe2+), Sol P = solution containing PEG@SiONCs and 4NA, Sol Q = Solution containing PEG@SiONCs and 4NP. The control represents the PL intensity of only PEG@SiONCs without any interfering entity. It can be seen from Fig. 7 that the PL intensity of PEG@SiONCs exhibits minimal reduction in the presence of a mixture of 12 probable interfering compounds (containing various functional groups excluding –NO2) represented by Sol X. However, with the addition of PA in Sol X, the PL intensity decreased considerably, and a quenching of ∼60 % occurred when the molar ratio between the concentration of each compound comprising Sol X and that of PA was maintained as 2:2. In another set of experiment, interference test was conducted in the presence of a mixture of –NO2 containing compounds represented by Sol Y. The PL intensity of PEG@SiONCs along with compounds in Sol Y was found to be ∼80 % of the control. A notable decrease in the PL intensity, exceeding 60 %, was observed upon the incorporation of PA into the sample, when the molar ratio between the concentration of each constituent compound (in Sol Y) and PA was adjusted to 2:2. Similarly, PL intensity of PEG@SiONCs in the presence of a mixture of 15 metal ions (represented by Sol Z) significantly quenched only on exposure to PA. At a molar ratio of 2:2 between the concentration of each metal ion (in Sol Z) and PA, the PL intensity of the nanocomposites reduced to more than 50 % of control. Additional sets of experiments were conducted to explicitly investigate the interference effect of 4NA and 4NP in PA sensing. The PL intensities of Sol P, which is a solution containing PEG@SiONCs and 4NA, and Sol Q, which is a solution containing PEG@SiONCs and 4NP, were determined to be approximately 70–80 % relative to the control. However, on exposure to PA (especially in a molar ratio of 2:2, i.e., [4NA]: [PA] (in Sol P) and [4NP]: [PA] (in Sol Q)), the PL intensity of the nanocomposites substantially decreased to ∼ 80 % and ∼70 % in Sol P and Sol Q, respectively. Nonetheless, these experiments provided conclusive evidence that the sensitivity of PEG@SiONCs for PA detection is not affected (or only minimally affected) by interference from various organic/inorganic compounds and metal ions.

In our earlier work [35], the as prepared PEG@SiONCs were explored for sensing of Cr(VI) ions. Therefore, to check the possibility of interference from Cr(VI) ions (source: K2Cr2O7), an additional experiment has been carried out to evaluate the interference of Cr(VI) in the sensing of PA by PEG@SiONCs. The resulting histogram (shown in Fig. S2) illustrates the change in PL of PEG@SiONCs in the presence of Cr(VI) ions as interfering species. The concentration ratios of Cr(VI) ions to PA were varied. It can be seen that the PL intensity of Sol R, a solution containing PEG@SiONCs and Cr(VI) ions, was determined to be ∼55 % relative to the control. However, on exposure to PA (especially in a molar ratio of 2:2, i.e., [Cr(VI)]: [PA], the PL intensity of the NCs substantially decreased to ∼ 80 %. From this experiment, it is clear that Cr(VI) ions may interfere with PA detection by PEG@SiONCs to some extent. Nonetheless, PEG@SiONCs still exhibit the capability to detect PA even in the presence of Cr(VI) ions. This could be attributed to the substantial overlap between the absorption spectrum of PA and the excitation spectrum of PEG@SiONCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on sensing capability
ce:section.ce:para: For various practical applications, it is crucial that the sensor's capability of sensing remains unaffected by the inherent environment or matrix of the analyte. In this context, it is important to note that the pH level of the medium is a significant determinant that can influence the sensor's performance. Therefore, the impact of pH on the sensing capability of PEG@SiONCs towards PA was monitored. It is to be noted that the default pH of the colloidal solution of PEG@SiONCs was ∼10.5. The PL quenching of PEG@SiONCs by PA was analysed at pH conditions, spanning from ∼2.0 to 12.0, adjusted using solutions of either HCl or NaOH as necessary. On the basis of this set of experiments, Stern-Volmer constant (KSV) values were determined at different pH conditions. The plot illustrating the effect of pH on the Stern-Volmer constant (KSV) values is shown in Fig. 8 .

It can be clearly seen that the variation in the KSV values is insignificant at a wider range of pH conditions. Moreover, it is also reflected from the KSV and Kq values at different pH conditions, as provided in Table 2 .
Certainly, the insignificant variation in the sensing ability of PEG@SiONCs for PA detection under acidic and basic conditions clearly reflect their robustness as a potential sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper strip-based sensing for practical applications
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assess the practical viability of utilizing PEG@SiONCs for real-time sensing of PA in field applications, a paper kit has been devised, as depicted in Fig. 9 . For the same, PEG@SiONCs extracted in the powder form were coated on a simple and cost-effective commercially available black coloured origami paper (generally used in art and craft activity). This particular paper was selected as it does not exhibit inherent PL or reflection when exposed to UV light. As can be seen from Fig. 9, the nanocomposite coated paper marked as “1” exhibited bright blue PL when exposed to UV light (∼365 nm). The paper strip marked “1” was used as a reference. Several such paper strips were prepared and subjected to varying concentrations of PA in the absence and presence of compounds containing various functional groups as potential interfering entities. For instance, paper strips marked from “2” to “9” were drop casted with solution containing progressively higher concentrations of PA, ranging from 12.4 to 172.0 μM. Insignificant quenching was observed when paper strips were exposed to a concentration of 172.0 μM PA. However, noticeable quenching of PL becomes apparent as the concentration of PA reaches 240.0 μM (as indicated by the paper labeled “10″). The paper strip labeled as “11” shows almost complete quenching of PL upon exposure to a concentration of 291.3 μM of PA. Based on these observations, it can be concluded that the visible detection limit of PA utilizing PEG@SiONCs coated paper strips is determined to be 240.0 μM.

Apart from this, interference of other compounds containing –NO2 groups on the visible detection of PA was also investigated. The purpose of conducting this experiment was to replicate a real-life scenario in which samples are likely to contain multiple compounds that could potentially interfere with the detection of PA. Hence, nanocomposite coated paper strips labeled from “12” to “16” were subjected to drop casting using a solution of –NO2 containing compounds (2NB, NPZ, NM, DNB, NB, 2NP, DNHPA, 4NA, 4NP) with each one having an effective concentration of (12) 55 μM, (13) 104.7 μM, (14) 150.0 μM, (15) 191.0 μM, (16) 229.2 μM. Although, a decrease in the PL was observed in these paper strips case compared to the reference marked “1”. Nevertheless, despite the presence of a combination of compounds containing –NO2 groups, each with significantly high concentrations of up to 229.2 μM, the PL remains unquenched. With an addition of 240.0 μM PA into this mixture, and subsequent drop casting it on the paper strip resulted in almost complete quenching of PL as can be seen from label (17).

The aforementioned findings demonstrate the real-time applicability of PEG@SiONCs coated paper strips for direct (or visibly) detection of PA at concentrations equal to or exceeding 240 μM in samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PA in real water samples: recovery test
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing of PA in actual water samples was performed using PEG@SiONCs. Two types of water samples were utilized: drinking water and an unfiltered water sample obtained from an open water source. Both water samples were spiked with a known concentration of PA, and then exposed to PEG@SiONCs. As anticipated, the PL of NCs quenched, and the corresponding concentration of PA was determined by using the calibration plot of F0/F versus [PA]. Table 3 presented below demonstrates substantial recovery rates, showcasing excellent reproducibility and precision, with a Relative Standard Deviation (RSD) below 5 %.

These observations strongly suggest that the as synthesized PEG@SiONCs hold substantial promise for effectively detecting PA in environmental water samples. Additionally, PEG@SiONCs exhibit excellent colloidal stability [35]. It can be realized from the insignificant variations in the PL intensity of NCs monitored over several days, as can be seen from Fig. S1 . Clearly, it proves the robustness of PEGylated Silicon oxide nanocomposites as non-toxic and cost-effective probes for PA detection.

Silicon and its nanocomposites are currently being subjected to extensive exploration due to its vast array of applications across diverse domains encompassing healthcare, energy, and the environment. One such application is sensing of hazardous/toxic chemicals and metal ions. In the current work, blue photoluminescent PEG@SiONCs have been explored for the highly selective sensing of PA. The advantages offered by these NCs over other luminophores (in PA detection) can be outlined as follows: (1) as these NCs are Si-based, they possess inherent advantages such as being non-toxic, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly, (2) excellent colloidal stability for at least 6 months under ambient conditions, (3) insignificant variation in their PA sensing capability across a wide pH range (pH 2.5 to 12), (4) utilizing electron-beam assisted approach in the synthesis of NCs itself offer various advantages including scalability, facileness, time-efficient synthesis (takes place in few seconds), no requirement of external toxic reducing agents, and other harsh conditions (i.e., high temperature and pressure), (5) highly selective PA sensing in 100 % water medium, amongst 13 metal ions and 21 nitro (‒NO2) compounds as well as other organic/inorganic compounds containing different functional groups. The sensing was based on PL “turn off” mechanism via IFE. An additional notable aspect of this study involves the demonstration of NCs coated paper strip-based sensing, which enables the direct monitoring of PA through the utilization of a portable UV lamp. This development holds significant potential for practical applications. Lastly, ongoing efforts are being made in our laboratory to improve the PL properties of radiolytically prepared Si-based NCs in order to enhance their sensing capabilities.
The funding source of this work is Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Trombay, Mumbai, India.
